           for 1973 necessitate changes in the formulas used for         income tax liability is based on the standard deduction
           computing the spendable earnings series issued by the         allowable under the income tax law; no change is being
           Bureau of Labor Statistics. The social security tax rate      made in the computation of the income tax. The social
           has been raised from 5.2 percent in 1972 to 5.85 per-         security tax rate (5.58 percent) is applied to gross aver-
           cent in 1973, and the maximum base on which this              age weekly earnings up to $207.69 beyond which the
           tax is computed has been raised to $10,800 from               maximum amount of $12.15 is assumed to be applicable.
           $9,000 in 1972. The increases in tax liability resulting      Separate spendable earnings series are derived for a

                      Data relate to employees in such occupations in the telephone industry as switchboard operators; service assistants; operating room instructors; and pay-station attendants. In 1971, such

                   t As noted on page 7 of the January 1972 issue of this magazine, the series does not include earned pay withheld as a consequence of delays caused by the wage freeze in effect between Au-
             gust 15 and November 14, 1971, and by administrative procedures of the Pay Board. These delays resulted from compliance with the Government's Economic Stabilization Program. Because these
             withheld payments were substantial and fell due under a significant number of new labor-management contracts in that industry, they affected a large proportion of SIC 372. Accordingly, the series
             will be supplemented by publication in the future of a series which will include withheld payments in the months earned.

               1
                  Computed by dividing the number of vacancies by the sum of employment plus vacancies and multiplying that quotient by 100.
               2
                  Long-term job vacancies are those vacancies that have remained unfilled for 30 days or more. The long-term job vacancy rate is computed by dividing the number of long-term vacancies by the sum of employ-
              m plus all job vacancies and multiplying that quotient by 100.

                                                                                 133                                    HOUSEHOLD DATA
                                                                                                                    SEASONALLY ADJUSTED



                                       Revised Seasonally Adjusted Labor Force Series


                                                  The seasonally adjusted series which appear on the pages that follow cover
                                            256 major series on the labor force 16 years and over. They include employment
                                            status by sex and age, by color, sex, and age, by occupation, by full- and part-
                                            time status, and for Vietnam Era veterans and nonveterans by age; employment
                                            by major industry group and by class of worker; nonagricultural persons at work
                                            on full- or part-time schedules: unemployment by duration and by reason; and
                                            unemployment rates by marital status, for household heads, and by industry.

                                                  There are a large number of other seasonally adjusted series which have
                                            not been included in this issue due to space limitations. These additional series
                                            are either components or aggregates of the series presented and may be obtained
                                            from the Bureau of Labor Statistics upon request.

                                                   The majority of the seasonally adjusted labor force series shown are com-
                                            puted by a method of aggregation. The process begins with the computation of
                                            seasonal factors and seasonally adjusted data for approximately 150 basic labor
                                            force series. These basic series are then aggregated according to a set of
                                            specifications to yield other seasonally adjusted estimates. All civilian labor
                                            force and unemployment rate statistics, as well as the major employment and
                                            unemployment estimates are computed by this method of aggregation. Totals
                                            of one aggregation pattern (e. g. , civilian labor force for persons 16 years and
                                            over aggregated by sex and age) will not necessarily equal those of any other
                                            pattern (e. g. , civilian labor force 16 years and over aggregated by color, sex,
                                            and age) . Since the annual averages are also a product of the aggregation pro-
                                            cedures, they may differ in some instances from those published elsewhere. A
                                            list of the basic series which are independently seasonally adjusted is available
                                            from the Bureau of Labor Statistics upon request.

                                                  In the past, revised seasonally adjusted monthly labor force series have
                                            been published in the February issue of Employment and Earnings from the point
                                            of inception of each series (on a conceptually consistent basis) through the most
                                            recent year. Because data for 1966 and prior years have now been "frozen" and
                                            thus will no longer be subject to change, future publication of seasonally adjusted
                                            labor force series in the February issue of Employment and Earnings will be
                                            limited to data beginning in 1967. Users of these data should note this change
                                            in publication policy and retain this issue of Employment and Earnings as the
                                            permanent source of pre-1967 seasonally adjusted data.

               Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies, the

                Two types of data collection schedules are used: Form BLS         employed are persons who are laid off, on leave without

           Labor Turnover. These schedules are of the "shuttle" type,

                                                Unemployment Insurance Data

               Insured unemployment represents the number of persons          as a percent of average covered employment in a 12-month
           reporting a week of unemployment under an unemployment in-         period ending 6 to 8 months prior to the week of reference.
           surance program. It includes some persons who are working part     Initial claims are notices filed by those losing jobs covered by an
           time who would be counted as employed in the payroll and           unemployment insurance program that they are starting a
           household surveys. Excluded are persons who have exhausted         period of unemployment. A claimant who continued to be
           their benefit rights and workers who have not earned rights to     unemployed a full week is then counted in the insured un-
           unemployment insurance. In general, excluded from coverage         employment figure.
           are those persons engaged in agriculture, domestic service, un-
           paid family work, selected nonprofit organizations, State and         Because of differences in State laws and procedures under
           local government and self-employment. Also excluded from           which unemployment insurance programs are operated, State un-
           the insured unemployment count, but included as employed in        employment rates generally indicate, but do not precisely mea-
           the household survey, are those persons who earned no wages        ure, differences among the individual States. Persons wishing to
           during the payroll period because they were temporarily absent     receive a detailed description of the nature, sources, inclusions
           from their jobs due to taking time off, illness and industrial     and exclusions, and limitations of unemployment insurance data
           dispute as well as unpaid vacations. The rate of insured un-       should address their inquiries to Manpower Administration,
           employment is the number of insured unemployed expressed           Washington, D.C. 20210.
